title: System driven out-of equilibrium by weak contacts with reservoirs
abstract: The non-equilibrium behavior of particle systems driven by reservoirs has been extensively studied in recent years. In one dimension, various regimes have been explored depending on the coupling strength to the reservoirs. In this paper, we investigate the role of the dimension and of the geometry of the contacts with the reservoirs. For the symmetric simple exclusion process with point contact reservoirs, we show that in dimension 2, as in one dimension, three different regimes occur depending on the coupling strength. On the other hand in dimensions 3 and higher, there exists only a weak coupling regime which is very sensitive to the microscopic structure of the contacts. We then argue that for reservoirs with mesoscopic size contacts the macroscopic fluctuation theory remains in force and we propose an extension of the additivity principle for multiple mesoscopic reservoirs.
summary: This paper explores how the dimensionality of a system affects the transport properties of particles driven by reservoirs. It identifies a breakdown of universality in 3D for microscopic contacts but proposes that mesoscopic-sized reservoirs restore predictable macroscopic behavior.
main_problem: Investigating how the dimensionality and geometry of reservoir contacts influence the current and non-equilibrium behavior of particle systems, specifically addressing why universality fails in 3D for point contacts.
main_result: The authors show that in 2D, three coupling regimes exist, whereas in 3D and higher, only a weak coupling regime exists that is sensitive to microscopic contact structure; however, universality is restored for mesoscopic reservoirs.
method: The study utilizes the Green function approach, scaling analysis of reservoir rates, and the macroscopic fluctuation theory (additivity principle) to analyze steady-state density and current.
model_or_system: The Symmetric Simple Exclusion Process (SSEP) on a d-dimensional lattice connected to reservoirs at specific sites or mesoscopic regions.
key_observables: Mean current (J), particle density profiles, and current fluctuations (large deviations/cumulants).
important_parameters: Dimension (d=1, 2, or >=3), coupling strength (A, B), reservoir densities (rho_a, rho_b), and contact size (point vs. mesoscopic).
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on the SSEP model in a steady state and assumes the validity of the additivity principle for mesoscopic reservoirs.
figures_summary: Figure 2 illustrates three mesoscopic reservoirs in a bulk, showing localized density variations near the reservoirs and a constant bulk density.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the SSEP model and reservoir dynamics, moves to a dimension-dependent analysis of coupling regimes (1D, 2D, 3D), explores current fluctuations and large deviations, and concludes with an extension of the additivity principle for multiple mesoscopic reservoirs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. In this figure, a square domain D is depicted with 3 holes in gray rep- resenting 3 reservoirs at different densities ρ1, ρ2, ρ3 and with different shapes. The flux Q(1) [0,τ] through the boundary ∂D1 of the first reservoir is represented by arrows. On the boundary of the square, one could consider either Neumann boundary con- ditions (if there is no incoming flux) or another reservoir.

Figure 2. Three mesoscopic reservoirs with densities ρ1, ρ2, ρ3 are depicted, the variations of the density are located in disjoint small circles around the reservoirs (dashed lines). In the rest of bulk, the density is essentially constant, equal to ¯ρ. For t and L large, the 3 reservoirs act as independent reservoirs in contact with the same bulk whose density ¯ρ has to be optimised. The large deviation functional is no longer mapped to the unit segment but to a star shaped geometry.
